text: Key parts of the oil market are suddenly awash in supply, as a stream of cargoes out of the Strait of Hormuz accelerates after the US-Iran agreement to open the waterway. https://t.co/wtgG5GtvwF
Is every day WTI crude oil stays above $65 still a great day?

Oil Prices Retreat to Pre-War Level as Supply Concerns Ease

Crude oil prices and calendar spreads have largely reverted to pre-war levels as traders anticipate a rapid normalisation of exports through the Strait of Hormuz and the smooth replenishment of depleted oil inventories around the world.

Front-month Brent crude futures retreated to $77 per barrel on June 23, down from a wartime high of $126 and the lowest since the United States and Israel launched a surprise attack on Iran at the end of February.

Prices have retreated fastest for contracts nearest to expiry causing the backwardation to soften and the futures curve to flatten ...

Heavy downside pressure is slamming the base metals complex. On the macro side, it's getting choked out by the USD rally, but the real pain is coming from CTAs aggressively puking shorts across the board.

Yesterday (June 23rd), CTAs stepped on the gas, accelerating systematic selling across the major venues. Kpler data shows CTAs flushed roughly 11,475 lots out of Comex copper and clipped around 2,834 lots from LME copper.

They didn't stop there—they dumped about 1,352 lots of LME aluminum, flipping net short for the first time since September 2025, and they’re continuing to hit the bid in both tin and nickel.

#copper #aluminum

Amazon Prime Day household spending is down 16%, per Bloomberg

EIA inventory report:

U.S. commercial crude inventories fell by 6 million barrels, while the Strategic Petroleum Reserve declined by a further 9 million barrels to a fresh multi-decade low. Combined, total U.S. crude stockpiles have fallen by around 100 million barrels since late April.
Inventories at Cushing, Oklahoma, the delivery hub for WTI futures, dropped to just 19 million barrels, the lowest level in 12 years and a threshold increasingly associated with operational constraints.
Gasoline and distillate inventories rose by 2 million and 3 million barrels, respectively, supported by robust refinery utilization and softer demand. Four-week average implied gasoline demand fell to the lowest seasonal level in 12 years, excluding the pandemic-affected period in 2020.
Total crude and refined product exports slowed to 12.1 million barrels per day, while U.S. crude production held steady at 13.8 million barrels per day.
Oil prices reset as stranded Gulf barrels head for market https://t.co/QG50uLoja3 via @saxobank

Trump is now turning his attention to refiners and fuel producers, criticizing the slow pace of declines in retail gasoline prices following the sharp drop in crude oil. However, the refining sector's response remains constrained by historically low inventories of refined products, particularly in the middle distillate complex.

As the charts highlight, US gasoline crack spreads remain elevated despite the recent easing in crude prices. The biggest beneficiary of the reopening of Middle East export flows has so far been the distillate market, with gasoil and diesel cracks falling from their March peaks. This reflects the fact that much of the additional crude now reaching the market is medium-to-heavy Gulf crude, which yields a higher proportion of middle distillates such as diesel, gasoil and jet fuel.

While increased crude availability should eventually help ease refining margins and lower fuel prices, depleted product inventories accumulated during the Strait of Hormuz disruption continue to support cracks. The combination of peak summer demand for gasoline keeping prices at the pumps seasonally elevated, refiners – for now - remain incentivized to maximize diesel and jet fuel production, delaying a more pronounced decline in gasoline prices.

text:
Good Morning! 

Futures are up, with ES +27

Oil prices are lower this morning, with WTI crude down to $71 a barrel. 

The techs are looking better this morning. 

South Korea's KOSPI regained ground after yesterday's tech driven sell-off. 

The market is waiting on $MU earnings which will drop later today after the bell. This report will be a critical indicator of semiconductor demand and AI spending. The market can see a big move in the AI tech stocks, led by chips tomorrow. 

$NVDA is leading the Mag 7 this morning. 

$GOOGL will join the Dow Jones Industrial Average, replacing Verizon $VZ, effective Monday, June 29, expanding mega cap tech presence in the blue chip average.

$TWLO is up 4% pre-market on renewed analyst optimism, including a Goldman Sachs Buy initiation with a $300 target, building on the their raised 2026 outlook and AI platform momentum following a successful turnaround.

The AI chip supply chain is facing a critical bottleneck at the manufacturing and testing layer, where optical interconnect scaling and silicon photonics capacity are emerging as key constraints on AI data center buildout. This is bullish for $ALAB $CRDO $AAOI $CIEN $COHR $FN $GLW $LITE. 

I don't see any other notable pre-market activity or significant news. 

I expect to see very little action in the market ahead of $MU earnings later today. 

The market may trade flat and range bound with very little volume for most of the day, with some movement at the end of the day as people do some last minute positioning ahead of those earnings.

US Stocks Set to Recover After Sharp Tech Selloff as Focus Shifts to Micron Earnings

U.S. stock futures are pointing higher Wednesday morning as markets look to stabilize following Tuesday’s sharp tech-led decline. The selloff was driven by weakness in semiconductor and big tech stocks, but investors are now turning their attention to $MU’s upcoming earnings.

Futures Snapshot:
- E-Mini S&P 500 up 0.25% at $7455.75
- E-Mini Nasdaq 100 up 0.58% at $29838.00
- E-Mini Dow down 0.03% at $52064.00

Oil Prices:
- WTI Crude down 1.86% near $71.85
- Brent Crude down 1.69% at $75.50

Key Market Context:
Markets are attempting a recovery after a difficult Tuesday session in which chip stocks and several Magnificent Seven names came under heavy pressure. The rebound in futures is being supported by bargain hunting ahead of $MU’s third-quarter results, which are due after the market close and are seen as a major test for the AI and memory chip trade.

Premarket Movers:
- $MU is up nearly 4% as investors position ahead of earnings. The stock had dropped more than 13% on Tuesday.
- Cerebras Systems is down more than 14% in premarket despite beating expectations in its first earnings report as a public company.
- FedEx is down around 7% after delivering mixed results and cautious full-year guidance.
- Twilio is up 5% after Goldman Sachs initiated coverage with a Buy rating and a $300 price target.
- Wendy’s is surging nearly 14% amid retail investor interest and short-squeeze speculation.

Asia Market Update:
South Korea’s Kospi Index is rebounding strongly after Monday’s roughly 10% plunge. The benchmark rose 3.26% on Wednesday as local investors bought the dip in technology stocks. The Kospi remains one of the best-performing major indices globally this year, up more than 100%.

Economic Calendar:
Today’s key data releases include:
- U.S. new home sales for May (10:00 a.m. ET)
- U.S. leading economic indicators for May (10:00 a.m. ET)
- Weekly crude oil inventories (10:30 a.m. ET)
- $70 billion 5-year Treasury note auction result (1:00 p.m. ET)

Overall Market View:
Sentiment is showing signs of stabilization after Tuesday’s tech rout, with investors shifting focus toward $MU’s earnings as a potential catalyst for the semiconductor and AI sectors. While the broader market remains cautious, the combination of oversold conditions in some tech names and expectations around $MU’s results is supporting a modest recovery in futures. Markets will be watching closely to see whether $MU can deliver strong enough results to restore confidence in the AI trade.

WTI crude oil taps early-March lows near $71/bbl. The shipping recovery in the Strait of Hormuz and slowing global demand are crushing any rebound momentum. 

🟢 Goldman Sachs (The Moderate):
Expects WTI to target ~$75 by Q4, with a full-year average range of $70–$78. They see high-level volatility persisting as geopolitical wildcards linger.

🔴 JPMorgan (The Bear):
Sticking firmly to its bearish outlook, forecasting a major slide down to $55–$58.

🐦 Z Pulse

• Oil Collapse: Brent crude falls to $75, lowest since February. Trump orders DOJ probe into oil companies for price manipulation while prices crater. Conflicting narrative on who's inflating what.

• Turkey MSCI Ultimatum: MSCI demands transparency reforms (ownership disclosure, market surveillance) by November 2026 or Turkey faces potential index removal. Heavy accusations per local analysts.

• $MU Earnings Watch: Micron reports today, market in wait-and-see mode. AI chip sector whipsawed after $NVDA AGM and margin concerns at peers like $CBRS. Space stocks ($ASTS, $RKLB) struggling while AI infrastructure attracts capital.

• AI Chip M&A: $QCOM acquires Modular Inc. for ~$4B to enable cross-platform AI software deployment. SK Hynix Nasdaq listing July 10, targeting $30B+ raise for memory chip capacity.

📰 News Flow

• Iran-Dollar Talks: Treasury Secretary Bessent says US-Iran negotiations include Tehran invoicing oil in dollars. Goldman cuts Q4 crude forecast to $80 citing US-Iran interim peace deal. IAEA dismisses "war of words," expects resumed inspections.

• Data Center Power Rush: $META and $MSFT lead $850B boom in data center leases. Multiple partnerships announced: $TSLA/$RUN team on AI data center energy, FuelCell/$FCEL partners with Fit Energy, Canadian nuclear firm AtkinsRealis seeks US approval for AI power tech.

• China Mineral Controls: Beijing detains two Japanese nationals over alleged rare earth smuggling, launches whistleblower hotline for export violations. Escalating minerals trade enforcement amid tech rivalry.

📊 Market Direction Read

• Bias: Cautious — Futures mixed, oil plunging, tech recovering from 3%+ $NDX selloff but awaiting $MU earnings as "gut check" for AI trade.

• Triggers:

• $MU earnings (afternoon) — memory pricing/AI demand signal for entire chip sector
• Oil volatility — Brent at $75, Trump DOJ probe vs. Iran deal conflicting narratives
• DXY above 101 pressuring gold, crypto ($BTC testing 59-60k support)

• Risk: $MU disappointment could reignite AI infrastructure skepticism after margin pressure at $CBRS; oil collapse may signal demand weakness beyond geopolitics.

🎯 On Watch Today

$MU earnings after close. Dan Ives (Wedbush) calls it a "gut check moment" for markets. Memory chip pricing and AI capex outlook will set tone for semiconductor complex heading into month-end.

Goldman Sachs cuts Q4 crude forecast to $80.

→ Gulf oil exports rebound to 60% of pre-war levels.
→ Markets question need for large security premium.
→ EV surge raises doubts on oil demand recovery.

Watch energy stocks, inflation trends, and currency moves.

🛢️ Goldman Sachs slashes Q4 crude oil forecast to $80/barrel, citing 3 key factors driving rapid price decline — analyst Daan Struyven, co-head of global commodities research, explains. #Gold #XAUUSD #Forex

JP Morgan just cut its Brent crude outlook for late 2026.

→ Q3 forecast: $86/barrel (down from prior)
→ Q4 forecast: $80/barrel
→ Year-end target: $78/barrel
→ Cites weak OECD inventory draws and lower oil demand

• JP Morgan Lowers Brent Oil Outlook For 2H26 And 2027

JP MORGAN:
LOWERS BRENT OIL OUTLOOK FOR 2H26 AND 2027

• FORECASTS BRENT AVERAGING $86/BBL IN 3Q26 AND $80 IN 4Q26, EXITING 2026 AT $78, AND AVERAGING $64 IN 2027

text:
#Oil falls as more supply flows through Hormuz; Brent #crude at below $77/bbl 
https://t.co/htkMpSd6Gu

Evening Market Pulse — June 24, 2026

A -4.56% drop in crude oil to $69.87 brings much-needed relief to your summer gas prices as key shipping lanes prepare to reopen.

The global energy map is shifting fast, and it is starting to take the pressure off the consumer.

Here is the read on the stock market today:

▪ The Strait of Hormuz is reopening after the signing of the Iran agreement, clearing the way for a major boost in global energy supply.

▪ UAE exports have recovered to nearly 85% of pre-war levels according to the EIA, helping WTI crude erase almost all of its March and April gains.

▪ The S&P 500 slipped -0.1% to 7,358.22 as semiconductor weakness and a rotation out of tech offset a 1.16% gain in the industrials sector.

Will this sharp drop in oil prices today be enough to cool core inflation before the Federal Reserve makes its next interest rate decision?

#OilPrices #Crude #Inflation #StockMarket

Market Brief:

💡 Semiconductor sector displays late-cycle behavior. AI optimism may fuel unsustainable valuations.

💡 $MU earnings due post-bell. WTI crude prices falling.

💡 Trading Tip: Strategies feeling like 'free money' often underperform the market long-term.

 https://t.co/VcCFfZL12S

3  things moved markets this June simultaneously: the SpaceX IPO extending gains past a $2 trillion valuation, a U.S.-Iran peace framework sending oil to a 3 month low, and the S&P 500 pushing toward record highs with a 1.9% intraday gain.

WTI crude dropped to around $80 a barrel as the Strait of Hormuz reopened to tanker traffic.

Energy stocks fell. AI, semiconductors, and travel stocks surged.

Micron and Western Digital led semiconductor gains on the back of AI infrastructure optimism.

The setup heading into the second half of 2026 is genuinely complicated.

The risk-on sentiment is real and the catalysts are real a major geopolitical de-escalation, a landmark IPO, and continued AI spending momentum all in the same week.

But the 30 year Treasury yield recently hit a 19 year high, the Fed hasn't signaled a pivot, and valuations on growth names are pricing in near perfect execution.

Markets can be right and expensive at the same time for longer than most people expect.

The question worth asking is whether the good news is now fully in the price.

Brent Crude held just above 73 per bbl

Futures Contract for Fall 2026
Crude Oil Brent Nov '26 (SCX26) = 73.37

If stabilized around 73, this implies Gasoline Prices at the Pump should be around $3 per Gallon (or Less) for Texas and the US South-East Region

𝐓𝐑𝐔𝐌𝐏 𝐍𝐀𝐌𝐄𝐒 𝐄𝐗𝐗𝐎𝐍, 𝐂𝐇𝐄𝐕𝐑𝐎𝐍, 𝐒𝐇𝐄𝐋𝐋, 𝐀𝐍𝐃 𝐁𝐏: “𝐆𝐀𝐒 𝐒𝐇𝐎𝐔𝐋𝐃 𝐁𝐄 $𝟐.𝟐𝟓 — 𝐓𝐇𝐄𝐘’𝐋𝐋 𝐁𝐄 𝐈𝐍 𝐁𝐈𝐆 𝐓𝐑𝐎𝐔𝐁𝐋𝐄”

Trump’s Iran deal crushed oil prices. Brent crude dropped under $77 a barrel. West Texas Intermediate sits under $73. The war premium that had driven gas above $4.50 nationally evaporated. But when Americans pull into a gas station, they’re not seeing it. The national average is still $3.93 (AAA, June 24). Trump called it exactly what it is.

At a press conference, a reporter asked about the DOJ investigation Trump had ordered. Trump didn’t hedge. “𝘚𝘰, 𝘪𝘵’𝘴 𝘌𝘹𝘹𝘰𝘯𝘔𝘰𝘣𝘪𝘭. 𝘐𝘵’𝘴 𝘊𝘩𝘦𝘷𝘳𝘰𝘯. 𝘐𝘵’𝘴 𝘚𝘩𝘦𝘭𝘭. 𝘐𝘵’𝘴 𝘉𝘗. 𝘐𝘵’𝘴 𝘢 𝘭𝘰𝘵 𝘰𝘧 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘮.” He said the oil prices “𝘩𝘢𝘷𝘦 𝘤𝘰𝘮𝘦 𝘥𝘰𝘸𝘯 𝘴𝘰 𝘮𝘶𝘤𝘩” and the pump prices have not followed commensurate with the move, adding: “𝘞𝘦 𝘴𝘩𝘰𝘶𝘭𝘥 𝘣𝘦, 𝘪𝘯 𝘮𝘺 𝘰𝘱𝘪𝘯𝘪𝘰𝘯, 𝘢𝘵 $2.25 𝘳𝘪𝘨𝘩𝘵 𝘯𝘰𝘸 𝘢𝘵 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘱𝘶𝘮𝘱.” When he warned the companies about the probe, he was direct: “𝘐 𝘩𝘰𝘱𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘺’𝘳𝘦 𝘯𝘰𝘵 [𝘨𝘰𝘶𝘨𝘪𝘯𝘨], 𝘰𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘳𝘸𝘪𝘴𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘺’𝘳𝘦 𝘨𝘰𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘵𝘰 𝘣𝘦 𝘪𝘯 𝘣𝘪𝘨 𝘵𝘳𝘰𝘶𝘣𝘭𝘦. 𝘞𝘦’𝘳𝘦 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘨𝘰𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘵𝘰 𝘱𝘭𝘢𝘺 𝘨𝘢𝘮𝘦𝘴.”

The math behind the claim: 19 million barrels per day now flowing through a Hormuz that was closed to traffic just weeks ago. That’s a 𝐡𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐢𝐜 𝐟𝐥𝐨𝐨𝐝 𝐨𝐟 𝐨𝐢𝐥 𝐬𝐮𝐩𝐩𝐥𝐲 hitting global markets. When crude drops 15-20% but retail gas drops less than 10%, the spread has to go somewhere. It goes into margin. The DOJ spokesperson confirmed the investigation is underway, calling fuel pricing “𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘰𝘯𝘭𝘺 𝘢 𝘯𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯𝘢𝘭 𝘴𝘦𝘤𝘶𝘳𝘪𝘵𝘺 𝘪𝘴𝘴𝘶𝘦” but something that “𝘪𝘮𝘱𝘢𝘤𝘵𝘴 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘸𝘢𝘭𝘭𝘦𝘵 𝘰𝘧 𝘦𝘷𝘦𝘳𝘺 𝘈𝘮𝘦𝘳𝘪𝘤𝘢𝘯.”

Every administration since Reagan has watched oil companies pocket supply windfalls without passing them to consumers, and done nothing. Trump fought to break the Hormuz stranglehold, delivered the supply, and is now going after the companies who are 𝐤𝐞𝐞𝐩𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐠𝐚𝐢𝐧𝐬 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐩𝐮𝐦𝐩. The companies named are the biggest publicly traded oil majors in the world. None of them have commented publicly on the investigation.

𝐓𝐫𝐮𝐦𝐩 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐞𝐝 𝐈𝐫𝐚𝐧, 𝐝𝐫𝐨𝐩𝐩𝐞𝐝 𝐨𝐢𝐥 𝐩𝐫𝐢𝐜𝐞𝐬, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐧𝐚𝐦𝐞𝐝 𝐧𝐚𝐦𝐞𝐬. $𝟐.𝟐𝟓 𝐢𝐬 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐠𝐞𝐭.

We are being price gouged at the pump! 

In 2020–2021, when WTI crude oil was around $68 per barrel, U.S. national average regular gasoline prices were typically in the $2.80. https://t.co/VuNY2BMORF

Oil is sharply lower today as increasing signs of a pick up in Hormuz shipping are seen. Brent Crude is fairly close to February levels now although refined fuels prices remain more elevated.  

This will be important new information for the RBNZ as they chart the path forward for the OCR in July and beyond.  We will have an updated set of forecasts to account for this available this afternoon.

It doesn't matter how much you grovel, or how hard you try to spin this whole disaster into something it's not, Trump deserves NO CONGRATULATIONS for gas prices slowly stabilizing to higher levels than before the war, and only after that SAME WAR caused a global energy crisis unlike anything we've seen in recent history.

Americans have collectively paid tens of BILLIONS more for gas than they otherwise would have.

The war itself has already cost the American taxpayer at least $100 billion. And this doesn't include the hundreds of billions more in costs ranging from inflationary expenses, higher prices for gas, petroleum, heating and electricity, food, fertilizer, air travel, shipping and consumer goods, as well as rising prices for aluminum as a result of pauses to shipments, feeding directly into automotive, aerospace, electronics, construction, and consumer product packaging costs.

Don't forget climbing mortgage rates in response to inflation threats either.

Then there's the cost of future military operations and repairs and restorations to overseas bases and depleted stockpiles, the economic fallout of a global energy disaster, the cost of farmers going bankrupt, and the indirect cost to taxpayers due to numerous economic setbacks around the world arising out of the geopolitical shitstorm Trump created.

And that doesn't even scratch the surface!

The US is setting up opportunities for Iran to cash in on massive windfalls.

The Trump administration has signaled that it's going to waive Iranian banking and oil sanctions; allowing them to sell oil globally at free market rates. The revenue Iran stands to gain from this will exceed the value of all other formal arrangements.

Moreover, according to JD Vance, the IRGC has seen the error of its ways, and both him and Jared Kushner have indicated that, alongside unfrozen assets, the US will establish a $300 BILLION "reconstruction" fund for Iran that will include private investments from around the world, which will no doubt come with its own kickbacks for Trump's allies 

Kushner himself said it would help "corral international investment in iran"

Basically, the US intends to bring Iran into the international club. 

Donald Trump went from calling Obama's JCPOA one of the worst deals he's ever seen, to walking head first into an agreement that's materially worse in every way, and not just on matters of nuclear proliferation, but in ways that normalize relationships with Iran and unlock massive financial benefits for the Iranian regime.

Before the war, Trump foolishly dismissed concerns about Strait of Hormuz closures, even though he was explicitly warned that Iran would resort to these countermeasures that have caused ongoing global trade disruptions, rising oil prices, and major production setbacks.

We've also managed to anger both our European and Middle East allies due to the extent of the impact of this global energy crisis.

Energy costs rose around the world. Massive oil infrastructure has been damaged or destroyed.

Oil production and global trade will continue to face significant and long-term disruptions.

Nearly every country around the world is dealing with the blowback of this crisis.

Whether the war ends or not, the economic toll will still be devastating.

Major oil producers have been forced to shut in production, meaning that they can't just magically turn the faucet back on.

Tankers also move at a very slow pace and will take a long time to move new oil shipments around the world.

It will take months, even years for energy production to return to prior levels. The cost of crude oil and gas won't be suddenly restored to their pre-war levels either.

Or as the saying goes, "rockets and feathers." 

Because when the cost of crude oil goes up, gas prices skyrocket quickly, but when the cost of crude oil goes down, gas prices float down slowly like a feather.

It doesn't matter how much you grovel, or how desperately you try to spin this whole disaster into something it's not, Trump deserves NO CONGRATULATIONS for gas prices slowly stabilizing to higher levels than before the war, and only after that SAME WAR caused a global energy crisis unlike anything we've seen in recent history.

Americans have collectively paid tens of BILLIONS more for gas than they otherwise would have.

The war itself has already cost the American taxpayer at least $100 billion. And this doesn't include the hundreds of billions more in costs ranging from inflationary expenses, higher prices for gas, petroleum, heating and electricity, food, fertilizer, air travel, shipping and consumer goods, as well as rising prices for aluminum as a result of pauses to shipments, feeding directly into automotive, aerospace, electronics, construction, and consumer product packaging costs.

Don't forget climbing mortgage rates in response to inflation threats either.

Then there's the cost of future military operations and repairs and restorations to overseas bases and depleted stockpiles, the economic fallout of a global energy disaster, the cost of farmers going bankrupt, and the indirect cost to taxpayers due to numerous economic setbacks around the world arising out of the geopolitical shitstorm Trump created.

And that doesn't even scratch the surface!

The US is setting up opportunities for Iran to cash in on massive windfalls.

The Trump administration has signaled that it's going to waive Iranian banking and oil sanctions; allowing them to sell oil globally at free market rates. The revenue Iran stands to gain from this will exceed the value of all other formal arrangements.

Moreover, according to JD Vance, the IRGC has seen the error of its ways, and both him and Jared Kushner have indicated that, alongside unfrozen assets, the US will establish a $300 BILLION "reconstruction" fund for Iran that will include private investments from around the world, which will no doubt come with its own kickbacks for Trump's allies 

Kushner himself said it would help "corral international investment in iran"

Basically, the US intends to bring Iran into the international club. 

Donald Trump went from calling Obama's JCPOA one of the worst deals he's ever seen, to walking head first into an agreement that's materially worse in every way, and not just on matters of nuclear proliferation, but in ways that normalize relationships with Iran and unlock massive financial benefits for the Iranian regime.

Before the war, Trump foolishly dismissed concerns about Strait of Hormuz closures, even though he was explicitly warned that Iran would resort to these countermeasures that have caused ongoing global trade disruptions, rising oil prices, and major production setbacks.

We've also managed to anger both our European and Middle East allies due to the extent of the impact of this global energy crisis.

Energy costs rose around the world. Massive oil infrastructure has been damaged or destroyed.

Oil production and global trade will continue to face significant and long-term disruptions.

Nearly every country around the world is dealing with the blowback of this crisis.

Whether the war ends or not, the economic toll will still be devastating.

Major oil producers have been forced to shut in production, meaning that they can't just magically turn the faucet back on.

Tankers also move at a very slow pace and will take a long time to move new oil shipments around the world.

It will take months, even years for energy production to return to prior levels. The cost of crude oil and gas won't be suddenly restored to their pre-war levels either.

Or as the saying goes, "rockets and feathers." 

Because when the cost of crude oil goes up, gas prices skyrocket quickly, but when the cost of crude oil goes down, gas prices float down slowly like a feather.

Few things that I gloss over here but could spend way more time going into depth are 

Britain and France were literally discussing how to divide parts of the Ottoman Empire before WWI had even ended. A lot of people point to the Sykes-Picot Agreement as one of the origins of modern Middle Eastern borders and many of the resentments that followed. 

the U.S. and UK helped overthrow Iran’s democratically elected Prime Minister in 1953 after he nationalized Iran’s oil industry. That’s a huge reason many Iranians view Western involvement very differently than Americans do. Depending on where you’re standing, it’s either protecting strategic interests or foreign interference in a sovereign country.

People often think Iran vs Saudi Arabia is just religion. It’s really religion + geopolitics. Iran wants to be the dominant regional power. Saudi Arabia wants to be the dominant regional power. The Sunni/Shia divide adds fuel to a rivalry that already existed.

The Middle East isn’t important just because of oil reserves. Roughly 20% of the world’s petroleum consumption passes through a tiny waterway.. the infamous Strait of Hormuz. If that shipping lane gets disrupted, the entire planet feels it as you know. 

Hezbollah isn’t just a militia. It’s also a political party. It has seats in Lebanon’s government. It runs social programs. It has a military force stronger than many countries. Which is why Lebanon’s politics are so complicated.

ISIS wasn’t just a terrorist organization. At its peak it controlled territory the size of the UK and governed millions of people. It collected taxes, operated courts, controlled oil fields, and attempted to function as a state.

Around 30-40 million Kurdish people live across Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Iran. They’re described as the largest ethnic group in the world without their own country. Modern borders left them divided across multiple states, and Kurdish independence movements have shaped regional politics for decades.

Obviously I didn’t mention the Cold War at all. But…

The thing  that keeps happening in Middle East history is countries partnering with groups because they solve an immediate problem… Then spending the next 20 years dealing with the unintended consequences. It happens over and over and over again.

Americans have collectively paid tens of BILLIONS more for gas than they otherwise would have.

The Hang Seng Index opened 84 points higher at 23,420, then fluctuated between 23,250 and 23,400 in the morning session before rising significantly in the afternoon, reaching a high of 23,565, a gain of 229 points. It closed up 75 points, or 0.32%, at 23,412. The Technology Index rose 79 points, or 1.81%, to 4,479. Total market turnover was HK$322.9 billion.

Hong Kong stocks ended a five-day losing streak, but the outlook remains pessimistic. The Hang Seng Index is expected to face significant resistance at 23,800, with a high probability of testing the 22,986 technical level. The Hong Kong stock market exhibits the "May slump, June crash" pattern, with the Hang Seng Index repeatedly searching for a bottom, reflecting continued market weakness. Only a loosening of monetary policy by banks after the July half-year closing offers a chance for a market rebound. With traditional large-cap tech stocks like AT&T and MJ showing weakness, funds have now shifted to speculating on new tech stocks such as chips and memory cards, while traditional tech stocks are no longer the market focus. One bank lowered its year-end target for the Hang Seng Index from 30,800 to 27,100 points.

European stock markets were mixed, with British and French stocks rising 0.31% and 0.54% respectively, while German stocks fell 0.62% due to pressure on defense stocks.

As more oil tankers passed through the Strait of Hormuz, New York crude oil futures fell below $70 per barrel for the first time since early March. Coupled with declining US Treasury yields, the Dow Jones Industrial Average opened slightly lower by 6 points on Wednesday, then rallied as much as 581 points to a high of 52,248, less than 100 points below its all-time high. The S&P 500 rose as much as 0.85%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite rose as much as 0.99% before falling as much as 0.91%. The S&P 500 also initially rose before declining.

U.S. stocks closed with the Dow Jones Industrial Average up 182 points, or 0.35%, to 51,848; the S&P 500 fell 7 points, or 0.1%, to 7,358; and the Nasdaq Composite dropped 110 points, or 0.43%, to 25,476.

The U.S. dollar index rose as much as 0.39% to 101.8, and the Bloomberg Dollar Spot Index rose 0.38%, hitting a seven-month high. The euro fell 0.5% to $1.1325; the yen fell 0.17%, hitting a low of 161.85 against the dollar. Bitcoin fell as much as 6.4% to $59,029. Spot gold prices fell below $4,000 per ounce for the first time since November last year.

Below: Satellite imagery shows smoke billowing from the Crimean Bridge spanning the Kerch Strait, which appears to have been attacked by a Ukrainian drone. (AFP)

Oil Falls to Postwar Lows
by Bloomberg  C. Charleston, A. Longley, G. Levin  | Wednesday, June 24, 2026
Oil's wartime price gains have evaporated as the US and Iran log progress in peace talks and more tankers openly transit the Strait of Hormuz, easing fears of a supply crunch.

West Texas Intermediate fell to settle near $70 a barrel, the lowest since the end of February just before the US and Israel launched their war on Iran. Brent, the global benchmark, settled below $74 a barrel, also the lowest since the war broke out.

"Oil is dropping sharply as the Strait of Hormuz remains open and traders start to focus on OPEC, including Iran, potentially moving to maximum production," Infrastructure Capital Management chief executive Jay Hatfield said. "We forecast that WTI could drop below $60 over the next two months as production ramps and stocks rebuild."

Vessels are transiting Hormuz with their satellite signals switched on, indicating growing confidence among shipowners about safe passage of the chokepoint, through which about a fifth of global oil supplies transited before the war. The International Maritime Organization also said it had received safety guarantees allowing hundreds of ships to exit the Persian Gulf.

Washington and Tehran have both flagged early progress in talks to end the war, although negotiations are likely to be protracted and claims from the two sides have diverged. In a sign of how much oil has been leaving Hormuz in recent weeks, the International Energy Agency estimates that the United Arab Emirates is exporting oil at nearly 85% of pre-war levels.

The resumed flows out of the Persian Gulf come as US data showed domestic crude inventories plunged to the lowest since 1984. Stockpiles at Cushing, Oklahoma, dropped below 19 million barrels, according to the Energy Information Administration, slipping below levels many traders view as operational minimums. Still, prices were little changed after the release, with traders focusing on broader signs of easing supply tightness.

A closely watched oil market indicator flipped into a bearish structure on Wednesday for the first time since February, with Brent's prompt time-spread trading in a shallow contango, with the nearest contract's price below the next month's. That structure typically signals expectations of oversupply.

"I think the physical markets are much weaker than most people anticipated in Asia and Europe with Brent now going contango as well," TP ICAP analyst Scott Shelton said. "This subtracts buyers from the oil market which makes it fall much more easily."

There's also been a collapse in prices for real-world barrels, with premiums for barrels from the North Sea to West Africa tumbling.

Oil prices are down about 40% from their high during the peak of the conflict, with the steady increase in traffic adding to workarounds that were put in place to mitigate disruption through Hormuz at the height of the war. The UAE alone has sold around 60 million barrels from inside the Persian Gulf in recent weeks.

The Republican-led Senate voted Tuesday to end the US war with Iran in a rare symbolic rebuke of US President Donald Trump. While the resolution is unlikely to force any changes in the administration's strategy, it represents the latest sign that the president lacks domestic support for the effort.

Separately, Trump said in a social media post he had ordered the Department of Justice to look into why gasoline prices haven't fallen faster as oil drops. The national average retail price has declined 14% since late May and is now below $4 a gallon, although it remains above the five-year seasonal average, according to data from the American Automobile Association. US retail diesel prices fell below $5 a gallon for the first time since mid-March.

Oil Falls to Postwar Lows Despite historically low US crude inventories, traders focused on improving Middle East supply flows and growing signs of potential oversupply.

U.S. SESSION: HOUSING CRACKS, OIL TANKS DESPITE SUPPLY SQUEEZE

May new home sales plunged 7.3% to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 580,000 units – well below the 638,000 forecast and down from April's upwardly revised 626,000.

The median new home price held at $424,900, but the average price rose 5.0% year-over-year. Prices aren't falling – buyers just can't afford them.

🛢️ EIA CRUDE INVENTORIES: BEAT THE ESTIMATE, BUT OIL STILL CRASHED

Headline: 
Crude inventories fell -6.1M barrels (forecast -3.9M, previous -8.3M). Total commercial stocks dropped to 412.1M barrels – about 7% below the 5-year average. 

Strategic reserves fell to 331.2M barrels – the lowest since 1983. Cushing inventories hit their lowest since October 2014.

Yet WTI crashed over 4.67% to ~$70, breaking below the $70 level.

The disconnect: 
Refineries are processing oil at record rates, and exports remain elevated. But gasoline inventories rose +2.064M barrels (forecast -0.578M) and distillates rose +3.064M barrels (forecast -0.505M). 

Finished products are piling up faster than consumers can use them – suggesting weaker domestic demand.

The bigger driver: 
the U.S.-Iran peace deal has unwound the wartime risk premium. The IMO has confirmed safety guarantees for tankers crossing the Strait of Hormuz. WTI is now trading roughly 40% below its conflict peak.

It’s a breakdown of the key Asian session economic data releases from the table (Japan & Australia):

🇯🇵 Japan – SPPI y/y (Services PPI)

· Actual: 3.3% (in line with forecasts)

· Previous: 3.4% (revised down slightly)

· Takeaway: No surprise here. The slight dip from prior isn't large enough to shift BoJ policy expectations, so JPY reaction is muted.

🇦🇺 Australia – Mixed but mostly weaker data

1. Retail Sales m/m (implied by the -0.7% figure)
   · Actual: -0.7% (sharp miss vs -0.4% forecast)
   · Previous: +0.4%
   · Takeaway: A significant contraction in consumer spending. This is bearish for AUD.

2. Monthly CPI y/y (table labels it "CPI m/m")
 
· Actual: 4.0% (below 4.3% forecast & below 4.2% previous)

· Takeaway: Headline inflation cooled more than expected.

3. Quarterly CPI y/y
· Actual: 4.0% (below 4.3% forecast & down from 4.4% previous)

· Takeaway: Confirms disinflationary trends, reducing RBA rate-hike pressure.

4. Trimmed Mean CPI q/q (core inflation)
· Actual: 0.4% (beat 0.3% forecast; previous 0.3%)

· Takeaway: Core inflation ticked up slightly, offering a tiny offset to the weak headline.

Overall Session Sentiment:

· AUD is under pressure – weak Retail Sales combined with softer headline CPI outweigh the slight core beat, reinforcing dovish RBA expectations.

· JPY is neutral – the SPPI print changed nothing.

In FX, expect AUDJPY to face downside risks following this mix.

US @SecScottBessent told CNBC today. "The Iranians will be invoicing in dollars. Everything we are doing is pushing the dollar back. We're reinforcing it." 

I have been writing for months that the American strategic objective in Iran was NOT nonproliferation. It was the protection of Eurasia corridor AND the US dollar hegemony. The Treasury Secretary just said it on national television for the FIRST TIME...

Bookmark to read or listen later -> https://t.co/FcOsHMvHqo

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘀𝗶𝘀

Iran was the LAST major OPEC producer NOT invoicing in US dollars. Iran sold crude to China in yuan. To India in rupees. To Russia in roubles. To Pakistan in barter. 

The petrodollar architecture anchoring US monetary hegemony since 1974 had ONE major hole. The Iranian hole. 

Bringing Iran into dollar invoicing was the STRATEGIC PRIZE that justified the ENTIRE war effort by USA against Iran. The nuclear file was the public narrative. 

The US dollar was the ACTUAL objective.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗮𝗱 𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗰𝘀...

Iran's oil revenue at peak is somewhere around $80 to $100B per year. 

The US Treasury market is $28 TRILLION. 

Iran's oil alone CANNOT save the dollar and in fact it won't even be felt in the grand scheme of things. The number is too small. 

The oil sale is -> NOT <- the prize...

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗴𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱?

The prize is what Iran SITS ON. 

World's 2nd largest natural gas reserves. 
World's 4th largest oil reserves. 
5th largest rare earth deposits (the strategic minerals for every 21st-century supply chain). 
7th largest copper deposits. 

At least 8.5 MILLION tonnes of lithium discovered in 2023. ALL locked out of the dollar system for 47++ years.

Bringing Iran INTO the US dollar invoicing does NOT capture oil revenue.

It captures the entire 21st-century commodity supply chain Iran sits on.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗰𝗶𝗽𝗹𝗲 𝗶𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘇𝗲

Bessent did NOT say "Iran will give us $24B." He said the IRANIANS WILL BE INVOICING IN DOLLARS. The principle. The architecture. The next  50 years of Iranian commodity sales priced, settled, and cleared in US dollars. 

The MOU is NOT a "peace agreement". 

The MOU is a DOLLAR REINTEGRATION AGREEMENT disguised as a ceasefire.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗨𝗦 𝗼𝗯𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝘃𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗡𝗢𝗧 𝗺𝗲𝘁

Bessent confirmed the PRINCIPLE. The IMPLEMENTATION is NOT complete. Three structural conditions remain unmet.

ONE. The Oman-Iran joint statement of June 23 establishes joint Hormuz administration with "services and costs in accordance with international standards." Can be denominated in dollars OR in a basket. NOT settled.

TWO. The emerging Iran-Pakistan-Turkey-Qatar bloc operates in regional currencies. Pakistan's CPEC trade with China runs in yuan. The bloc's internal trade has NOT been committed to dollar invoicing.

THREE. Iran's mineral exports are NOT yet in any dollar-clearing infrastructure. Chinese traders pay in yuan today. The transition requires NEW banking that does NOT yet exist.

The next phase of this conflict will NOT be fought with bombs. It will be fought with payment rails, clearing systems, AND central bank balance sheets.

The Iran war was ALWAYS about preventing the de-dollarisation architecture from completing. The Iranian oil revenue is NOT what saves the dollar. 

The Iranian GROUND is what saves the dollar. The next 24 months reveal whether the dollar reintegration completes OR whether the emerging Muslim bloc absorbs Iran into a parallel commodity-clearing architecture the US cannot reverse without another war.
